fastapi.q: Audit FastAPI for deprecation drift. Find every API marked deprecated/legacy/scheduled-for-removal in this codebase (look for DeprecationWarning, warnings.warn, @deprecated, 'deprecated' docstrings or comments, Annotated[..., 'deprecated']). For each, report: where it is defined, what marker labels it deprecated, and whether any other internal FastAPI module or test still actively imports/calls it. Highlight any item that is BOTH marked deprecated AND still referenced internally — that's the drift.

Map FastAPI's user-input trust boundaries. List every type of caller-controlled HTTP input (path param, query, body, header, cookie, file, form) and identify the single chokepoint where it is parsed and validated before reaching user-defined endpoint functions. Then identify any places where input bypasses validation (e.g., passes Request directly, raw body access, custom request classes). Be specific with file paths.

Compare the public surface of FastAPI to its test coverage by name. Read the names re-exported from `fastapi/__init__.py` (top-level names, not modules). For each name, identify whether `tests/` has a test file directly named after it (test_<name>.py or test_<name>s.py). List names with NO matching test file as the public-surface-without-test gap.

requests.q: Audit every way a caller can disable or weaken TLS verification when using `requests`. Enumerate: (a) the `verify=` parameter and its accepted values, (b) Session-level defaults and inheritance, (c) environment variables (REQUESTS_CA_BUNDLE, CURL_CA_BUNDLE), (d) custom adapters or transport overrides, (e) any HTTP-only behavior. For each, cite the code path and explain what failure mode it produces. Flag any that silently degrade security without warning.

When `requests` follows a redirect across hosts/schemes (HTTPS → HTTP, or to a different host), what happens to: the Authorization header, the Cookie header, and the request body for non-idempotent methods? Trace the redirect handling code to identify where each is preserved, stripped, or rewritten. Highlight any case that could leak credentials cross-host.

Audit `requests` for shared-mutable-state risks. Find any module-level mutable singletons, class-level mutable defaults, mutable default function arguments, or per-Session caches that could cause one user's state to bleed into another's, or that could cause subtle bugs when the user mutates the value they got back. Cite file paths.

sqlmodel.q: Inspect `sqlmodel.main.get_sqlalchemy_type()` and the related type-mapping logic. Enumerate every Python type explicitly mapped to a SQLAlchemy type. Then identify common Python types that DEVELOPERS WOULD REASONABLY EXPECT to work but are NOT in the explicit map (e.g., `decimal.Decimal`, `uuid.UUID`, `datetime.time`, `bytes`, JSON-typed `dict`/`list`, enums, `typing.List[T]`, `typing.Optional[T]`, generic types). For each gap, predict what happens at runtime: silent fallback, RuntimeError, NotImplementedError, or wrong type.

Identify every place in `sqlmodel` where importing a module, declaring a `class X(SQLModel, table=True)`, or instantiating a model causes a side effect on shared global state — e.g., SQLAlchemy declarative registry, the default metadata, a ContextVar, a mutable class attribute, or a cached lookup. List each trigger with file path; for each, explain the user-visible consequence (e.g., 'two test cases declaring `class User` at module level conflict in the registry').

Audit `sqlmodel` for places where user-supplied strings could end up as raw SQL — e.g., as table names, column names, ORDER BY columns, raw text() statements, or anywhere a string is concatenated into SQL. List any APIs that accept caller-provided identifiers and explain whether SQLAlchemy's quoting/binding makes them safe or whether the burden is on the user. Highlight risky surfaces.
